N.name: number of undecayed nuclei at time t

lambda.name: decay constant
lambda.note: Probability of decay per nucleus per unit time; higher λ means faster decay

t_half.name: half-life
t_half.note: Time for half the nuclei to decay; t½ = 0.693/λ = ln2/λ

A.name: activity (rate of decay)
A.note: A = λN = A₀e^(-λt); measured in Becquerel (1 Bq = 1 decay/s) or Curie (1 Ci = 3.7×10¹⁰ Bq)

tau.name: mean lifetime
tau.note: τ = 1/λ = t½/0.693; average time a nucleus survives before decaying

locked_facts: N = N₀e^(-λt) — exponential decay law; number of undecayed nuclei decreases exponentially with time
t½ = 0.693/λ = ln2/λ — half-life is inversely proportional to decay constant
A = λN = A₀e^(-λt) — activity (decay rate) also decays exponentially; A₀ = λN₀
After n half-lives: N = N₀/2ⁿ — simple halving rule for quick calculations
τ = 1/λ = t½/ln2 ≈ 1.44 × t½ — mean lifetime is 44% longer than half-life
Alpha decay: ²²⁶Ra → ²²²Rn + ⁴He (A decreases by 4, Z decreases by 2)
Beta-minus decay: ¹⁴C → ¹⁴N + e⁻ + ν̄ (neutron → proton + electron + antineutrino; Z increases by 1, A unchanged)
Gamma decay: excited nucleus → same nucleus + γ photon (no change in A or Z, only energy state changes)
truth_statements: Radioactive decay is a purely statistical process — it is impossible to predict WHEN a specific nucleus will decay; only the probability per unit time (λ) is known
Half-life is independent of the amount of substance, temperature, pressure, or chemical state — it is an intrinsic nuclear property
The exponential decay law N = N₀e^(-λt) is valid only for large numbers of nuclei; for individual nuclei, decay is random
Alpha particles are helium-4 nuclei (²He⁴), beta particles are high-energy electrons (β⁻) or positrons (β⁺), and gamma rays are high-energy photons
Carbon-14 dating uses t½ = 5730 years: measuring the ratio of ¹⁴C to ¹²C in organic remains determines age up to ~50,000 years

aha_moment.description: Student watches 1000 nuclei decaying in real-time. After exactly one half-life, approximately 500 remain — but NOT exactly 500 each time (statistical fluctuation). After 2 half-lives: ~250. After 3: ~125. The exponential curve emerges from pure randomness at the individual level. Each nucleus has no 'memory' of how long it has existed.
aha_moment.visual_cue: Grid of 1000 dots blinking out randomly; counter shows N vs t matching N₀e^(-λt) curve; vertical lines at t½, 2t½, 3t½ showing halving

STATE_1.title: Random Decay of Individual Nuclei
STATE_1.description: Display a grid of 200 nuclei (colored dots). Each has a probability λΔt of decaying in each time step. Watch nuclei randomly 'pop' (change color to grey). No pattern in which nucleus decays next — it is purely probabilistic. Count surviving nuclei in real-time.
labels: Each nucleus: P(decay) = λΔt per step
No way to predict which one next

STATE_2.title: Exponential Decay Curve Emerges
STATE_2.description: Plot N vs t as the grid decays. Despite individual randomness, the aggregate curve follows N = N₀e^(-λt) smoothly. Mark the half-life point where N = N₀/2. Show that after each half-life, exactly half of the REMAINING nuclei decay.

STATE_3.title: Three Types of Decay
STATE_3.description: Show three decay processes side by side: (1) Alpha: parent nucleus ejects He-4, becomes element Z-2; (2) Beta-minus: neutron converts to proton, ejects electron + antineutrino; (3) Gamma: excited nucleus emits photon, no transmutation. Show conservation of mass number and charge in each.

STATE_4.title: Interactive Exploration
STATE_4.description: Student adjusts decay constant λ (or equivalently half-life) and initial number N₀. Watch the decay curve change shape. Toggle between N vs t and Activity vs t plots. Carbon-14 dating mode: input remaining fraction → calculate age. Challenge: how many half-lives to reach 1% of original?

common_misconceptions.misconception: After two half-lives, all the radioactive nuclei will have decayed
common_misconceptions.correct: After two half-lives, N = N₀/4 (one-quarter remains), NOT zero. After n half-lives, N = N₀/2ⁿ. The substance NEVER fully decays to zero in finite time — it asymptotically approaches zero.
trigger_phrases: do half life ke baad kuch nahi bachega

common_misconceptions.misconception: A nucleus that has survived a long time is 'due' to decay and more likely to decay soon
common_misconceptions.correct: Radioactive decay is memoryless — the probability of decay per unit time (λ) is constant regardless of how long the nucleus has existed. A nucleus that has survived 10 half-lives has the exact same probability of decaying in the next second as a freshly created nucleus.
trigger_phrases: its been long it should decay

common_misconceptions.misconception: Heating a radioactive substance makes it decay faster
common_misconceptions.correct: Radioactive decay rate is completely independent of temperature, pressure, chemical state, or physical conditions. The decay constant λ is a nuclear property determined by the strong and weak nuclear forces inside the nucleus, which are unaffected by external conditions.
trigger_phrases: garam karo toh jaldi decay hoga

legend.active_nucleus: Bright colored dot — undecayed radioactive nucleus
legend.decayed_nucleus: Grey dot — nucleus that has already decayed
legend.decay_curve: Exponential curve N = N₀e^(-λt); steeper for larger λ
legend.half_life_line: Vertical dashed line at t = t½ where N drops to N₀/2
legend.activity_curve: A = λN curve; same exponential shape, scaled by λ

concept_relationships.relationship: Nuclei with low binding energy per nucleon relative to their neighbors are unstable and undergo radioactive decay to reach more stable configurations
Alpha decay is a type of nuclear disintegration; fission can be induced in heavy radioactive nuclei; many fission products are themselves radioactive
Gamma ray emission from excited nuclei is analogous to photon emission in Bohr model — both involve discrete energy level transitions
Radioactive decay is fundamentally probabilistic — the exponential law emerges from applying probability theory to large ensembles
